result:
:::writing
The town only appeared on maps when you weren’t looking for it.

If you searched deliberately—typed its name into a GPS, traced the county lines, zoomed in until the pixels blurred—it dissolved into something else: a stretch of road, a nameless river, a suggestion of trees. But if you drove without purpose, following the lazy logic of late afternoon light, you might find yourself passing a leaning sign that read WELLCROSS in chipped white paint, the letters spaced as if they had once tried to escape each other.

Eli found it by accident, though later he would suspect the town had found him first.

He had been driving for hours, chasing nothing in particular except distance. His phone had died somewhere outside of a gas station that sold boiled peanuts and imitation sunglasses. The sky was a low, colorless lid, and the road seemed to run not through the land but alongside it, as if both were reluctant companions.

He noticed the sign only because it looked temporary, like it might be removed at any moment. He slowed, then turned, more out of curiosity than intention.

The road into Wellcross was narrower than the highway, though it didn’t feel smaller. Trees leaned inward, their branches forming an uneven tunnel. The air shifted, subtly—cooler, maybe, or simply quieter. Eli rolled down his window and caught the scent of something he couldn’t place, something like rain remembered rather than rain itself.

The town revealed itself slowly.

A diner with a flickering sign. A row of houses painted in colors that seemed chosen long ago and never reconsidered. A clock tower at the center, its hands frozen at 3:17. There were people, but they moved with a kind of deliberate calm, as though they had all agreed on the same unspoken tempo.

Eli parked near the diner. The engine ticked as it cooled, a small, insistent sound that made the stillness around it feel even deeper.

Inside, the diner was warmer than expected. Not physically, but atmospherically—as if the air itself had weight. A woman behind the counter looked up as he entered. She didn’t smile, exactly, but her expression opened in recognition.

“You made it,” she said.

Eli paused. “I’m sorry?”

She gestured to a stool. “Sit. You look like you’ve been driving a while.”

“I have,” he said, taking the seat. “But I don’t think we’ve met.”

“Not yet,” she replied, pouring him a cup of coffee he hadn’t asked for.

It was good coffee. Not remarkable, not artisanal, just exactly what coffee should be. He drank it before he realized how much he needed it.

“What is this place?” he asked.

The woman considered the question as if it required calibration.

“Wellcross,” she said finally. “You saw the sign.”

“I mean…” He hesitated, searching for a way to articulate the unease that had settled into him. “It doesn’t feel like a place people plan to visit.”

“That’s because they don’t,” she said. “Not really.”

“Then how do they get here?”

She met his eyes. “Same way you did.”

Eli opened his mouth to respond, then closed it. He didn’t have a better answer.

A man at the end of the counter spoke without turning. “You passing through, or staying?”

Eli glanced over. The man was older, his face lined in a way that suggested not age, exactly, but accumulation—years layered unevenly.

“I don’t know,” Eli said.

The man nodded, as if that were the only reasonable response. “Most don’t.”

The woman set a plate in front of Eli. Eggs, toast, something that might have been bacon. He hadn’t ordered, but the smell anchored him, made everything else feel briefly negotiable.

“Eat,” she said. “It helps.”

“With what?”

She tilted her head, listening to something Eli couldn’t hear. “Deciding.”

He ate.

The food was simple, but each bite seemed to sharpen the edges of his thoughts. Memories surfaced—not in sequence, but in fragments. A conversation he’d left unfinished. A job he’d walked away from without explanation. A name he hadn’t said aloud in months.

He set down his fork.

“What happens if I stay?” he asked.

The woman wiped her hands on a towel that appeared from nowhere. “You live,” she said. “Same as anywhere else. You wake up, you go about your day, you find ways to fill the hours.”

“And if I leave?”

She shrugged. “You leave.”

“That’s it?”

“That’s it.”

The man at the end of the counter chuckled softly. “He means, what’s the catch.”

The woman glanced at him, then back at Eli. “There’s always a catch,” she said. “But it’s not the same for everyone.”

Eli leaned back, the stool creaking beneath him. “What was it for you?”

She smiled then, faintly. “I stayed.”

“That doesn’t answer the question.”

“It does,” she said gently. “Just not in the way you want.”

Outside, the light had shifted. It was impossible to tell how much time had passed. The clock tower still read 3:17.

Eli stood, suddenly restless. “I need some air.”

The woman nodded. “Don’t go too far.”

He stepped outside.

The town seemed unchanged, but something in his perception had altered. The colors were slightly more saturated, the shadows deeper. He walked toward the clock tower, drawn by its stillness.

At its base, there was a door.

It hadn’t been there before. He was sure of it.

He hesitated, then reached for the handle. It turned easily.

Inside, the air was cool and dim. A narrow staircase spiraled upward, disappearing into shadow. There was no sound except his own breathing.

He climbed.

Each step felt both immediate and distant, as if he were ascending through layers of something thicker than air. Halfway up, he noticed markings on the wall—names, carved or written or etched in ways that defied easy categorization.

Some were familiar. Not to him personally, but in the way certain names carry weight even when detached from context. Others were entirely unknown.

Near the top, he saw one that made him stop.

His own.

ELI, it read, the letters uneven, as if written in haste or uncertainty.

He stared at it, a strange vertigo pulling at him. “That’s not possible,” he whispered.

“Isn’t it?”

The voice came from above.

He climbed the remaining steps more quickly than he intended.

At the top, the staircase opened into a small room. Windows lined the walls, but instead of looking out onto the town, they revealed something else—roads, countless roads, stretching in every direction. Some were familiar, others impossibly foreign. All of them seemed to lead both toward and away from the tower.

A figure stood at the center.

It looked like him.

Not exactly—there were differences, subtle but undeniable. The posture was slightly off, the expression a fraction too calm.

“You took your time,” the figure said.

Eli’s mouth went dry. “What is this?”

The figure gestured to the windows. “This is where you decide which way your story goes.”

“I already make decisions,” Eli said, his voice sharper than he intended.

“Do you?” The figure tilted its head. “Or do you just keep driving until something forces you to stop?”

Eli flinched.

“That’s not fair.”

“No,” the figure agreed. “It’s not. But it’s accurate.”

Eli looked at the windows again. Each road seemed to pulse faintly, as if alive with possibility.

“What happens if I choose one?” he asked.

“You live it,” the figure said. “Fully. No hedging, no drifting. Just… living.”

“And the others?”

The figure smiled, not unkindly. “They stop being yours.”

Eli thought of the diner. The woman. The man. The town that appeared only when you weren’t looking for it.

“Why me?” he asked.

The figure shrugged. “Why anyone?”

Silence settled between them.

Eli took a step toward the windows, then another. One road, in particular, caught his attention. It looked ordinary—almost disappointingly so. A straight stretch of asphalt under a clear sky.

But there was something about it. A sense of continuity, maybe. Or simply the absence of escape.

He reached out.

“Wait,” the figure said.

Eli paused.

“You don’t have to choose,” the figure added. “You can go back down, leave the town, keep driving. No one will stop you.”

Eli considered that.

He imagined returning to his car, the engine turning over, the road unfolding ahead of him in its familiar, indefinite way.

He imagined the comfort of not deciding.

Then he looked at his name on the wall, carved in uncertain lines.

“I think,” he said slowly, “that’s what I’ve been doing this whole time.”

The figure nodded. “Yes.”

Eli took a breath, then placed his hand against the glass.

The world shifted.

When he opened his eyes, he was standing on the road.

Behind him, there was no town. No tower. Just a horizon that promised nothing and everything in equal measure.

He took a step forward.

This time, he didn’t hesitate.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
